title: Demystifying Objectivity with Operator Algebra Quantum Error Correction
authors: Marin Girard, Gong Cheng, ChunJun Cao
abstract: Quantum Darwinism extends the decoherence formalism to explain how classicality and objectivity emerge from quantum mechanics. However, existing approaches often capture only partial aspects of objectivity, leading to its mischaracterization and making it difficult to pin down precisely. By connecting quantum Darwinism to operator algebra quantum error correction, we show that the emergence of objectivity can be identified with the algebraic local recoverability of quantum codes. Applying this algebraic framework to stabilizer codes, we show that it yields a far more precise characterization of classicality and redundancy, unifies the traditional measures of objectivity, enables efficient classification via coding-theoretic tools, and supports large-scale Clifford simulations of decoherence dynamics.
summary: This work develops an algebraic framework to precisely define objectivity by linking it to the local recoverability properties within quantum error-correcting codes. By analyzing the structure of logical Pauli algebras on system fragments, the authors unify existing measures and provide quantitative criteria (SAO, LAO) for how classical information emerges from quantum entanglement.
main_problem: The paper aims to precisely characterize the emergence of 'objectivity' and 'classicality' from quantum mechanics, moving beyond partial descriptions like Quantum Darwinism.
main_result: Objectivity is rigorously identified with the algebraic local recoverability of quantum codes, providing a unified and computable classification scheme for classicality and redundancy.
method: The authors connect Quantum Darwinism to Operator Algebra Quantum Error Correction (QECC), using tools like analyzing the center of recoverable logical algebras ($Z(M_f)$) on stabilizer codes.
model_or_system: The analysis is performed within the framework of quantum error-correcting codes, specifically utilizing stabilizer codes defined by Pauli subgroups. The dynamics are simulated via 'brickwork circuits' modeling system-environment interaction.
key_observables: Objectivity/Classicality Measure ($Z(M_f)$ non-triviality), Quantum Mutual Information (QMI), Classical bits ($c$), and Quantum qubits ($q$).
important_parameters: Strong Algebraic Objectivity (SAO), Localized Algebraic Objectivity (LAO), Quantum Doped Objectivity (QDO); plateau widths related to code distances.
main_assumptions: The analysis heavily relies on the mathematical structure of stabilizer codes and uses Poisson approximations for calculating probabilities in random subsystem fragments.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate QMI plateaus vs. fragment size, depict different algebraic regimes (SAO, LAO, QDO), and show heatmaps mapping classical ($c$) versus quantum ($q$) information content over simulated time steps.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the problem using QD to establishing the connection with QECC algebra. It then develops theorems quantifying recoverability ($Z(M_f)$) and mutual information, culminating in proving equivalences between physical conditions (sQD) and algebraic structures (SAO).
why_interesting: It provides a deep mathematical link between fundamental concepts of quantum foundations (objectivity/classicality) and the highly structured tools of Quantum Error Correction Codes, which is crucial for understanding decoherence dynamics in complex systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a) Plot of max|f| I(R : f) and E|f|(R : f) for a [[120, 10]] CSS code with dZ = 1 and dX = 20, which captures the decoherence of a 10-qubit system. Maximization and aver- aging is done over contiguous fragments. b) Plot of the exact E|f|(R : f) for a [[30, 1]] CSS code with dZ = 1, dX = 15, alongside the full theoretical prediction (9) and sparse ap- proximation (10). The values of m(ϵ) Z and m(ϵ) X that bound the classical plateau up to ϵ precision as per Corollary 9 is shown in green.

Fig. 1 shows the bounds proposed by Corollary 9 on a plateau with ϵ = 0.1. Algebraic objectivity. In addition to their strong con- nection to existing QD concepts, the algebraic formula- tion provides precise tracking of both the type and the distribution of information — given f, one can efficiently determine the logical operators supported on it using our algorithm presented in the End Matter. With this im- proved precision, we can easily classify different strengths of objectivity and characterize them along our two cri- teria: classicality and redundancy. We highlight a few interesting classes and draw connections with existing lit- erature on QD.

FIG. 2. Algebraic regimes for a chosen fragment family F = {fi}. The horizontal axis is the number of bits that are ℓ- redundant for ℓ= O(|F|), the number of fragments, while the vertical axis is a typical local quantum rank of the recoverable algebra ¯q = ⟨qi⟩F. The blue/pink hue also encodes ℓand ¯q. Different regimes of algebraic objectivity occupy distinct regions of this diagram, separated schematically by dashed lines. The hatched region is forbidden, as entering it would require the fragment family to redundantly share noncentral logical information.

FIG. 3. Heat map of the algebra Mf for all contiguous frag- ments fj = {j −|f| + 1, j −|f| + 2, · · · , j} with |f| = 8 and two k = 2 sources broadcasting in a brickwork circuit con- struction. Each pixel encodes the algebraic content with teal for classical c information and pink for quantum information q. While classical information spreads out from both sources, fragments lose access to the quantum information quickly. We overlay the emergent light cone bound of each source for our brickwork circuit.

FIG. 4. Schematic brickwork Clifford circuit with periodic boundary conditions. Each layer is a product of disjoint local bricks, and adjacent layers are shifted by half a block. Match- ing labels at the two edges denote a periodic brick.
